findings.title: SQL injection via f-string table name interpolation
findings.code_snippet: await db.execute(text(f"DELETE FROM {table} WHERE agent_id = :aid"), {"aid": agent_id})
findings.description: The table name is interpolated via f-string into a raw SQL text() call. While the table names currently come from a hardcoded list (cleanup_tables), this pattern is inherently unsafe. If the list is ever sourced from user input, or if a developer adds a dynamically-constructed table name, this becomes a direct SQL injection vector. The CLAUDE.md security rules explicitly forbid SQL string concatenation. The agent_id parameter is properly parameterized, but the table name is not.
findings.suggestion: Replace the f-string interpolation with SQLAlchemy ORM delete statements using model classes. If raw SQL is unavoidable, use a whitelist validation: assert table in ALLOWED_TABLES before interpolation. Better yet, map table names to SQLAlchemy Table objects and use table.delete().where(table.c.agent_id == agent_id).

findings.title: Pending approvals count not scoped by tenant in stats endpoint
findings.description: The pending_approvals count in get_enterprise_stats is not filtered by tenant_id, unlike the other three metrics (total_agents, running_agents, total_users). This means an admin of tenant A sees the pending approval count from ALL tenants, leaking cross-tenant data. This violates the multi-tenancy invariant documented in CLAUDE.md: 'Every entity is company/tenant-scoped. All queries must filter by tenant_id.'
findings.suggestion: Add tenant scoping by joining through agents: select(func.count(ApprovalRequest.id)).where(ApprovalRequest.status == 'pending', ApprovalRequest.agent_id.in_(select(Agent.id).where(Agent.tenant_id == tid))). This matches the pattern already used in the list_approvals endpoint at line 225-226.

findings.title: Collaboration service lists agents across all tenants
findings.description: list_collaborators returns ALL agents across all tenants that are running or stopped, not just those belonging to the same tenant as the requesting agent. This is a tenant isolation breach. The docstring says 'Returns agents from the same enterprise (same creator's org)' but the implementation does not enforce this. An agent in tenant A can see and collaborate with agents from tenant B.
findings.suggestion: Add tenant_id filtering: select(Agent).where(Agent.id != agent_id, Agent.tenant_id == agent.tenant_id, Agent.status.in_(['running', 'stopped'])). The agent variable is already fetched from DB on line 81, so agent.tenant_id is available.

findings.title: Feature flag gate silently suppresses all exceptions
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
            logger.debug("Suppressed: %s", e)
findings.description: The feature flag gate in call_llm wraps the entire execution_engine_v2 routing decision in a bare except-Exception that logs at DEBUG level. If the execution engine itself throws a real error (DB connection failure, LLM client crash), the error is silently swallowed and the code falls through to the legacy path. The user never learns their feature-flagged engine failed, and operators will miss it unless DEBUG logging is enabled in production (it typically is not).
findings.suggestion: Log at WARNING level with structured context (agent_id, flag name). Consider only catching the specific import/flag-check exceptions, and letting execution engine errors propagate so they trigger the existing fallback logic or surface to the user.

findings.title: Comment says L3 approval but code is a no-op pass
findings.description: The comment states that sensitive tools in the restricted zone 'require L3 approval via autonomy check below', implying the code delegates enforcement to a later check. However, the autonomy boundary check below only fires if the tool_name appears in _TOOL_AUTONOMY_MAP. If a tool is in _SENSITIVE_TOOLS but not in _TOOL_AUTONOMY_MAP, neither the security zone nor the autonomy check will block it. The comment creates a false sense of security by implying enforcement that may not exist for all sensitive tools.
findings.suggestion: Either verify that every tool in _SENSITIVE_TOOLS also has an entry in _TOOL_AUTONOMY_MAP, or add explicit L3 enforcement inline: `return await _request_human_approval(agent_id, tool_name, ...)`.

findings.title: websocket_chat function is 510 lines with 6+ nesting levels
findings.code_snippet:
@router.websocket("/ws/chat/{agent_id}")
async def websocket_chat(
    websocket: WebSocket,
    agent_id: uuid.UUID,
    ...
findings.description: The websocket_chat handler at 510 lines is far beyond the 100-line P1 threshold. It handles authentication, session resolution, history loading, OpenClaw routing, quota checks, LLM call orchestration with abort logic, fallback model retry, task creation detection, and message persistence -- all in one function with nesting up to 6 levels deep. Cyclomatic complexity is estimated at 35+.
findings.suggestion: Extract into focused helpers: _authenticate_ws, _resolve_session, _build_conversation_context, _handle_openclaw_message, _handle_llm_message, _detect_and_create_task. The main handler becomes a ~50-line orchestrator.

findings.title: Token limit check silently suppresses DB failures
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
            logger.debug("Suppressed: %s", e)
findings.description: The agent token limit check (daily/monthly quota) catches all exceptions and logs at DEBUG level. If the database is down, the agent bypasses all token limits and runs unlimited. This is a safety-critical check (cost control) that silently becomes a no-op on infrastructure failure.
findings.suggestion: Fail open with a WARNING log that includes agent_id and error type. Alternatively, fail closed: if the quota check cannot complete, block the request with a clear error message.

findings.title: update_llm_model returns generic 500 error hiding root cause
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
        await db.rollback()
        logger.error("Failed to update LLM model %s: %s", model_id, e)
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Failed to update model")
findings.description: The outer catch-all in update_llm_model catches every exception type and returns a generic 500 with 'Failed to update model'. This masks errors that should be 400/409 (duplicate model name, invalid provider).
findings.suggestion: Handle specific exception types: IntegrityError -> 409 with conflict details, ValidationError -> 422, and only use 500 for truly unexpected errors.

findings.title: Mutable default arguments in SQLAlchemy model columns
findings.description: The Agent model uses mutable dict/list literals as default values for JSON columns. In Python, mutable default arguments are shared across all instances. Modifying one instance's dict before flush can mutate the default for future instances.
findings.suggestion: Use default=lambda: {"read_files": "L1", ...} (callable default) or use server_default=text("'{}'::jsonb") to push the default to the database.

findings.title: call_llm function is 275 lines with complex branching
findings.code_snippet:
async def call_llm(
    model: LLMModel,
    messages: list[dict],
    agent_name: str,
    ...
) -> str:
findings.description: call_llm at 275 lines handles token limit checks, system prompt building, vision format conversion, LLM client creation, tool-calling loop with round warnings, and token tracking. Cyclomatic complexity estimated at 25+. Duplicates logic that already exists in the cleaner execution_engine_v2.
findings.suggestion: Extract _convert_vision_messages, _run_tool_loop. Deprecate call_llm once execution_engine_v2 flag is fully rolled out.

findings.title: Security zone enforcement silently skipped on exception
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as e:
        logger.debug("Security zone check skipped for agent %s: %s", agent_id, e)
findings.description: The security zone enforcement in execute_tool catches all exceptions and logs at DEBUG level. If the database query for the agent's security_zone fails, the tool executes without any security zone restrictions. A 'public' agent that should only have read-only access could execute sensitive tools if the DB is temporarily unreachable. This is a security-critical fail-open pattern.
findings.suggestion: Fail closed: if the security zone check fails, block the tool execution. Default to the most restrictive zone ('public') on failure.

findings.title: update_permissions accepts untyped dict body with no validation
findings.code_snippet: data: dict,
findings.description: The update_agent_permissions endpoint accepts a raw dict as the request body instead of a typed Pydantic model. This bypasses all input validation. CLAUDE.md requires 'Validate all external input'.
findings.suggestion: Define Pydantic models: class PermissionUpdate(BaseModel): scope_type: Literal['company', 'user'] = 'company'; scope_ids: list[uuid.UUID] = []; access_level: Literal['use', 'manage'] = 'use'.

findings.title: Task execution launched as fire-and-forget asyncio.create_task
findings.code_snippet: _asyncio.create_task(execute_task(task_id, agent_id))
findings.description: execute_task is launched via asyncio.create_task with no error handling, no done callback, and no reference retention. If execute_task raises an exception, it will be silently lost.
findings.suggestion: Add a done callback that logs errors. Better: store the task reference. Best: use a proper task queue (Redis, Celery).

findings.title: create_agent handler is 172 lines with 4-level nesting
findings.code_snippet:
@router.post("/", status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED)
async def create_agent(...)
findings.description: The create_agent endpoint at 172 lines handles quota checking, tenant resolution, agent construction, lifecycle transition, participant creation, permissions, OpenClaw API key generation, file system initialization, skill copying, container starting, and audit logging.
findings.suggestion: Extract: _resolve_tenant_defaults, _setup_agent_permissions, _copy_skills_to_agent, _setup_openclaw_agent. Handler becomes a ~50-line orchestrator.

findings.title: enterprise.py router exceeds 685 lines with 7+ responsibilities
findings.code_snippet:
# LLM Model Pool, Enterprise Info, Approvals, Audit Logs,
# Dashboard Stats, Tenant Quotas, System Settings,
# Org Structure, Invitation Codes
findings.description: enterprise.py is a single router file handling 9 distinct domains at 685 lines. Exceeds the 400-line guideline and approaches the 800-line mandatory split threshold.
findings.suggestion: Split into focused router modules: api/llm_models.py, api/approvals.py, api/audit_logs.py, api/tenant_quotas.py, api/system_settings.py, api/invitation_codes.py.

findings.title: WebSocket tool_call persistence silently swallows save failures
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception as _tc_err:
                                print(f"[WS] Failed to save tool_call: {_tc_err}")
findings.description: When a completed tool call fails to persist to the database, the error is caught and printed (not logged via logger). Uses print() instead of structured logging.
findings.suggestion: Use logger.warning with structured context (agent_id, tool_name, conversation_id). Consider a retry mechanism.

findings.title: Agent model uses raw strings for status, agent_type, security_zone
findings.code_snippet:
agent_type: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(20), default="native")
agent_class: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(30), default="general")
security_zone: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(30), default="standard")
findings.description: The Agent model uses plain strings for agent_type, agent_class, security_zone, and status with no type-level constraints.
findings.suggestion: Define Python enums: class AgentType(str, Enum): NATIVE = 'native'; OPENCLAW = 'openclaw'. Use in both model and schema.

findings.title: Repeated 'Suppressed: %s' debug messages are ungreppable
findings.code_snippet: logger.debug("Suppressed: %s", e)
findings.description: At least 15 exception handlers across websocket.py and agent_tools.py use the identical message 'Suppressed: %s'. When diagnosing production issues, searching logs for 'Suppressed' gives no indication of which code path failed.
findings.suggestion: Give each log site a unique, descriptive prefix, e.g. 'chat_history json parse failed: %s', 'quota increment skipped: %s'.

findings.title: Agent model has 40+ columns but zero domain behavior methods
findings.description: The Agent model is a pure data container with 40+ columns and zero methods. Business logic is scattered across API handlers and utility functions.
findings.suggestion: Move domain behavior into the Agent model: reset_token_counters_if_needed, is_expired, transition_to, can_accept_messages, is_openclaw property.

findings.title: Inline comments on audit blocks restate the obvious
findings.description: Comments like '# Audit: agent created' appear before every audit block. The write_audit_event call with its event_type parameter already communicates this.
findings.suggestion: Remove per-block audit comments. Add a single module-level docstring explaining the audit pattern.

findings.title: update_agent_permissions accepts untyped dict body (type-design perspective)
findings.code_snippet: data: dict,
findings.description: The update_agent_permissions endpoint accepts a raw dict instead of a Pydantic schema. No validation occurs on scope_type, scope_ids format, or access_level. This bypasses FastAPI's automatic request validation. (Also flagged as P1 security by review-code.)
findings.suggestion: Define a Pydantic model: class AgentPermissionUpdate(BaseModel): scope_type: Literal['company', 'department', 'user']; scope_ids: list[uuid.UUID] = []; access_level: Literal['use', 'manage'] = 'use'.

findings.title: Section header comments removed without replacement
findings.code_snippet: async def _discover_resources(arguments: dict) -> str:
findings.description: Three section header comments removed in this diff. In a 4660-line file, these section dividers serve as navigation landmarks.
findings.suggestion: Either restore the section headers or extract function groups into separate modules (agent_tools_mcp.py, agent_tools_email.py).

findings.title: print() statements used instead of structured logger in main.py
findings.code_snippet: print(f"[Proxy] ss-local -> {node['label']} ({node['server']}:{node['port']})", flush=True)
findings.description: main.py uses 22 print() statements for startup logging instead of structured logging. Includes sensitive information like proxy server addresses.
findings.suggestion: Replace print() with logger.info()/logger.warning() using structured context. For proxy info, use DEBUG level.

findings.title: ConnectionManager.send_message silently drops messages on WS errors
findings.code_snippet: async def send_message(self, agent_id: str, message: dict):
findings.description: If any ws.send_json() raises an exception, the loop aborts and remaining connections never receive the message. No try/except around individual sends, no cleanup of dead connections.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each ws.send_json in try/except, log failures, and remove dead connections.

findings.title: Duplicate lazy token counter reset loop in list_agents
findings.code_snippet: # Lines 88-94 (admin path) and 128-133 (non-admin path) -- identical block
findings.description: The exact same 6-line token reset loop appears twice in list_agents (admin path and non-admin path).
findings.suggestion: Extract a helper: async def _reset_counters_for_agents(agents, db).

findings.title: call_llm_via_engine docstring lacks parameter documentation
findings.code_snippet: """Call LLM via the middleware-based execution engine."""
findings.description: A new 100-line function with 10 parameters (including callbacks) has only a single-line docstring. Future maintainers need the interface contract.
findings.suggestion: Add parameter documentation covering on_chunk, on_tool_call, on_thinking callbacks, return semantics, and '[Engine returned empty content]' edge case.

findings.title: AgentExecutionEngine middleware system not wired to primary entry point
findings.code_snippet: class AgentExecutionEngine:
findings.description: The primary chat flow still uses the legacy call_llm function. The engine and its middleware ecosystem are only used via feature flag, risking divergence from the main path.
findings.suggestion: Migrate the primary WebSocket chat flow to use AgentExecutionEngine as the sole execution path. Deprecate legacy call_llm.

findings.title: Tavily/Google/Bing API responses parsed without error handling
findings.code_snippet: data = resp.json()
findings.description: The search functions call resp.json() without checking HTTP status code first and without try/except around JSON parsing.
findings.suggestion: Check resp.status_code before parsing JSON. Wrap in try/except JSONDecodeError.

findings.title: Frontend API service uses 'any' type extensively
findings.code_snippet:
create: (data: any) =>
    request<any>('/agents/', ...),
findings.description: The agentApi and other API objects use 'any' for many inputs and responses, defeating TypeScript's type safety.
findings.suggestion: Define typed interfaces for each endpoint and progressively type the most-used endpoints first.

findings.title: Malformed tool_call history records silently skipped
findings.code_snippet:
except Exception:
                continue  # Skip malformed tool_call records
findings.description: Malformed conversation history records are silently skipped with a bare except-continue. No logging, no visibility into corruption rate.
findings.suggestion: Log a warning with message ID and error details to detect systematic data corruption.

findings.title: EnterpriseSettings duplicates the fetch wrapper from api.ts
findings.code_snippet: async function fetchJson<T>(url: string, options?: RequestInit): Promise<T> { ... }
findings.description: EnterpriseSettings.tsx defines its own fetchJson<T> helper that duplicates request<T> from api.ts. Differs in URL prefix and lacks auto-logout on 401.
findings.suggestion: Remove local fetchJson and route all calls through api.ts.

findings.title: ExecutionState and middleware protocol are well-designed
findings.description: Positive: Good design with typed dataclass, Protocol-based middleware, and cooperative cancellation. Minor: no __post_init__ validation.
findings.suggestion: Add __post_init__ to validate agent_id is not None and max_rounds > 0.

findings.title: collaboration.list_collaborators returns empty list on agent not found
findings.code_snippet:
if not agent:
            return []
findings.description: Returns empty list when agent_id does not exist. Caller cannot distinguish 'no collaborators' from 'invalid agent'.
findings.suggestion: Raise ValueError('Agent not found') or use a Result/Either pattern.

findings.title: Shadow variable 't' in list_agents template listing
findings.code_snippet: for t in templates
findings.description: Loop variable 't' could shadow i18n translation import if one is ever added.
findings.suggestion: Rename to 'tmpl' or 'template'.

findings.title: Removed comment 'Build rich prompt' was the only hint of why
findings.code_snippet: from app.services.agent_context import build_agent_context
findings.description: The diff removed the comment explaining what build_agent_context aggregates (soul.md, memory.md, skills, relationships).
findings.suggestion: Restore or rephrase: '# Build system prompt from agent soul, memory, skills, and relationships'.

findings.title: Redundant module-level logger shadowed by local import
findings.code_snippet:
import logging
_logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
findings.description: call_llm_via_engine creates a local _logger that shadows the module-level logger. Both have the same name.
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant import and _logger. Use the module-level logger.

positive_observations: Path traversal protection in agent_tools.py: file operations resolve paths and check is_relative_to / startswith against the workspace root, preventing directory traversal attacks
Proper parameterized queries throughout: agent_id parameters in SQL text() calls use :aid binding, not string interpolation
Consistent audit logging: all state-changing operations emit audit events with actor, tenant, resource context
Agent lifecycle state machine: transitions are validated through a formal state machine (domain/agent_lifecycle.py) preventing invalid state changes
Secrets encryption: LLM API keys are encrypted at rest via SecretsProvider before being stored in the database
Autonomy check in execute_tool correctly fails closed on exception -- blocks the operation and returns a clear error message
Audit event writes consistently use a try/except pattern with logger.warning and exc_info=True, reasonable for non-critical side effects
The execution engine properly uses reversed middleware order for on_error hooks and re-raises when no middleware handles the error
Agent deletion uses savepoints (begin_nested) for cleanup of related tables, preventing partial cleanup from poisoning the whole transaction
The execution engine uses a well-structured middleware pattern with Protocol typing, composable hooks, and cooperative cancellation via asyncio.Event
The agent lifecycle uses a dedicated state machine with TransitionContext, properly separating domain rules from the API layer
CollaborationService has a clean fallback pattern: Redis Streams event bus with file-based inbox fallback, ensuring message delivery even when Redis is down
Feature flags are cleanly implemented with proper Redis cache invalidation and admin-only access control
Systematic replacement of bare except: pass with except Exception as e: logger.debug/warning(...) across all files is a significant improvement for debuggability
The collaboration.py docstring was updated to accurately reflect the new Redis Streams implementation with file-based fallback
Good use of structured logging with contextual parameters (agent_id, table name) in the new warning/debug messages in agents.py delete flow
